nativeRange.habitat: Seaward coral reefs and reef slopes with mixed coral, rocky rubble, and patches of algae and sand; generally encountered in small groups foraging close to the substrate, with adjacent fine sand used as nighttime burrowing refuge.

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate at least 5 cm deep for nighttime burrowing
live rock with caves, ledges, and rubble pockets
open mid-water swimming space across the tank length
tightly fitting lid or fine mesh cover (jumper)

diet.notes: In the wild feeds on small benthic invertebrates — polychaetes, copepods, amphipods, and other crustaceans picked from rubble and algae. In captivity is generally an easy feeder, taking vitamin-enriched frozen mysis and brine shrimp, marine pellets, and quality flake food. Multiple small feedings per day tend to keep coloration and activity at their best.

avoidWith: ornamental shrimp (cleaner shrimp, peppermint shrimp, sexy shrimp)
small ornamental snails and feather-duster tubeworms
other Halichoeres or similar-shape wrasses in tight quarters
dragonets and mandarins (food competition for small inverts)

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe; 1580x1185; tack-sharp full-body adult in reef setting showing the diagnostic green head/red facial stripes and orange-red horizontal dotted body lines in full colour.
cello caruso-turiello; 2048x1365; sharp adult from a higher angle showing the reticulated green/red flank pattern and orange dashed lines; alternate view for variety.
Debra Baker; 1781x1328; subadult clearly showing the paired false dorsal eye-spots (ocelli) that give the species its name 'biocellatus'; slight blue cast but whole fish and diagnostic.

summary: The Red-Lined Wrasse (Halichoeres biocellatus) is a compact, peaceful reef fish typically reaching 10–12 cm, and is generally considered one of the more beginner-friendly wrasses, accepting frozen and prepared foods readily.
careNotes: The Red-Lined Wrasse is widely regarded as one of the more peaceful and beginner-friendly Halichoeres species, suited to mixed reef communities. Adults typically reach around 10 cm in aquariums, with a wild maximum closer to 12 cm total length. A tank of at least 250 L with a long footprint is generally advised, giving the fish open swimming room across the mid-water column — these are active foragers rather than rock-hugging fish, and smaller systems tend to feel cramped for a full-grown adult. A fine sand substrate of at least 5 cm depth is important: like other Halichoeres wrasses, this species dives into the sand to sleep at night and when startled, and a coarse or shallow bed can stress or injure it. A tightly fitting lid or mesh top is strongly recommended, as wrasses in this genus are well-documented jumpers. The Red-Lined Wrasse is generally considered reef-safe with corals and clams, but is best described as reef-safe with caution because hungry individuals have been reported to prey on ornamental shrimp (cleaner, peppermint, and sexy shrimp), small ornamental snails, feather-duster tubeworms, and other delicate invertebrates. Like most wrasses, this species is a protogynous hermaphrodite — individuals begin life female and the dominant fish in a group can transition to male — so a tank typically holds a single specimen unless the system is large enough to support a small harem. Juveniles display a pair of false eye-spots (ocelli) on the dorsal fin that fade with maturity; this trait is behind the species name 'biocellatus' and the alternate trade name 'False Lined Wrasse'. The species is also commonly sold as 'Biocellatus Wrasse' and 'Ocellated Wrasse'.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Labridae, Eupercaria), wild maximum size (12 cm TL), depth range (6–35 m), preferred temperature (25–28°C), Western Pacific distribution, and juvenile ocelli morphology.
